title: Ermine frosting
subtitle: Cooked flour buttercream — less sweet, silkier than American style
excerpt: Ermine frosting, also called flour buttercream or boiled milk frosting, starts with a cooked flour-and-milk paste that is then beaten into creamed butter. The result is lighter and less sweet than American buttercream, with a texture closer to whipped cream. It pipes well and holds at room temperature.
sourceNotes: Flour-based buttercream (sometimes called roux buttercream) is documented in American and British domestic baking guides from the early twentieth century as a lighter alternative to the all-sugar-and-fat versions. The cooked-starch technique appears in domestic science textbooks of the 1900s–1920s, where a thin cooked paste of flour and milk was beaten into creamed butter to produce a stable emulsion. Fannie Farmer, Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896), available via Project Gutenberg, documents similar cooked-starch approaches in frosting preparation. The name 'ermine' is a modern convenience label; the base technique is fully public-domain.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough to fill and frost a 2-layer 20 cm cake
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. The paste stage takes a little longer to thicken at double quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cooked paste can be made ahead and refrigerated for up to 3 days. Bring to room temperature and beat into freshly creamed butter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The cooked flour-and-milk paste at the heart of ermine frosting is a simple starch-thickened sauce (béchamel without the seasoning). Whisk the flour into cold milk before heating to prevent lumps, then cook over medium heat, whisking constantly, until the mixture thickens to a firm, smooth paste that holds its shape when you drag a line through it. Remove from the heat and press a sheet of cling film against the surface to prevent a skin forming. The paste must be at room temperature before it goes into the butter; warm paste melts the butter and produces a greasy, split frosting.

content.text: Ermine is noticeably less sweet than American buttercream and far lighter on the palate. It is also more delicate and should be used the day it is made. The paste stage takes ten minutes but sets the whole character of the frosting.
Cook the paste, cool to room temperature

Whisk the {{plain-flour}} and {{caster-sugar}} together in a small saucepan. Gradually whisk in the {{whole-milk}} until smooth. Place over medium heat and whisk constantly until the mixture thickens to a firm paste that holds a line. This is the
. Remove from the heat, press cling film against the surface, and leave to cool fully to room temperature (about 30 minutes). Do not rush this stage.

Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} with the {{vanilla-extract}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} for 3 to 4 minutes until pale and very fluffy. With the mixer running on medium, add the cooled paste a tablespoon at a time, allowing each addition to incorporate fully before adding the next. The frosting will look curdled briefly after each addition and then smooth out. Once all the paste is in, increase the speed to high and beat for 2 minutes until light and smooth.

Ermine frosting is best used within an hour of making. Spread or pipe as desired. A frosted cake keeps at cool room temperature for the day; refrigerate overnight and return to room temperature for an hour before serving.
